config.title: SQL Fundamentals for HR Analytics
config.description: Learn beginner SQL through realistic HR analytics tasks focused on payroll and performance data. Students practice selecting salary records, filtering review data, sorting compensation reports, calculating pay metrics, and summarizing workforce performance with `salary`, `performance`, and supporting `staff` data.
learnOutcomes: Select the right payroll and performance columns with SELECT.
Filter HR data with single conditions, AND, and BETWEEN.
Sort and limit salary and review reports with ORDER BY and LIMIT.
Calculate pay and label performance using arithmetic and CASE.
Summarize HR metrics with aggregate functions, GROUP BY, and HAVING.

questions.sectionDescription: Start by retrieving the right payroll and performance fields without mixing in later topics too early.

lesson.title: Select One Column
lesson.body:
You are working with HR payroll data. The first SQL skill is learning how to return just one field from one table.

Use SELECT to choose the column and FROM to choose the table.

SELECT base_salary
FROM salary;

questions.question: You are supporting the payroll officer who wants to inspect the base salary values recorded for the current payroll month. Return only the `base_salary` column from `salary`.

sqlTables.seedSql:
CREATE TABLE staff (
  staff_id INTEGER,
  first_name VARCHAR,
  last_name VARCHAR,
  department VARCHAR,
  job_title VARCHAR,
  hire_date DATE,
  city VARCHAR,
  manager_id INTEGER,
  employment_status VARCHAR,
  work_email VARCHAR
);

INSERT INTO staff VALUES
  (1, 'Amina', 'Bello', 'HR', 'HR Analyst', '2021-03-15', 'Lagos', 6, 'Active', 'amina.bello@hr.demo'),
  (2, 'David', 'Mensah', 'Finance', 'Payroll Officer', '2020-07-01', 'Accra', 6, 'Active', 'david.mensah@hr.demo'),
  (3, 'Grace', 'Njeri', 'HR', 'Recruiter', '2022-01-10', 'Nairobi', 1, 'Active', 'grace.njeri@hr.demo'),
  (4, 'Samuel', 'Okoro', 'IT', 'HR Systems Specialist', '2019-11-25', 'Abuja', 6, 'Active', 'samuel.okoro@hr.demo'),
  (5, 'Lydia', 'Hassan', 'Operations', 'Learning Coordinator', '2023-05-08', 'Lagos', 1, 'Leave', 'lydia.hassan@hr.demo'),
  (6, 'Peter', 'Adeyemi', 'HR', 'HR Manager', '2018-02-12', 'Lagos', NULL, 'Active', 'peter.adeyemi@hr.demo'),
  (7, 'Ruth', 'Mwangi', 'Finance', 'Compensation Analyst', '2021-09-20', 'Nairobi', 2, 'Active', 'ruth.mwangi@hr.demo'),
  (8, 'John', 'Musa', 'Operations', 'People Operations Associate', '2024-01-05', 'Abuja', 6, 'Probation', NULL);

CREATE TABLE salary (
  staff_id INTEGER,
  salary_month DATE,
  base_salary INTEGER,
  bonus INTEGER
);

INSERT INTO salary VALUES
  (1, '2026-04-01', 4200, 300),
  (2, '2026-04-01', 3900, 250),
  (3, '2026-04-01', 3100, 150),
  (4, '2026-04-01', 4800, 400),
  (5, '2026-04-01', 3300, 0),
  (6, '2026-04-01', 6500, 800),
  (7, '2026-04-01', 4100, 350),
  (8, '2026-04-01', 2800, 100);

CREATE TABLE performance (
  review_id INTEGER,
  staff_id INTEGER,
  review_period DATE,
  performance_score REAL,
  training_hours INTEGER,
  absence_days INTEGER,
  promotion_ready VARCHAR
);

INSERT INTO performance VALUES
  (1001, 1, '2026-03-31', 4.6, 18, 1, 'Yes'),
  (1002, 2, '2026-03-31', 3.8, 12, 2, 'No'),
  (1003, 3, '2026-03-31', 4.2, 20, 0, 'Yes'),
  (1004, 4, '2026-03-31', 3.5, 10, 3, 'No'),
  (1005, 5, '2026-03-31', 3.9, 14, 4, 'No'),
  (1006, 6, '2026-03-31', 4.8, 8, 0, 'Yes'),
  (1007, 7, '2026-03-31', 4.1, 16, 1, 'Yes'),
  (1008, 8, '2026-03-31', 3.2, 24, 2, 'No');

sqlHints: Select only one column from the `salary` table.

lesson.title: Select Multiple Columns
lesson.body:
Once you can return one field, the next step is returning several related fields from the same table.

SELECT staff_id, salary_month, bonus
FROM salary;

questions.question: You are helping the compensation analyst review the current payroll file. Return `staff_id`, `salary_month`, and `bonus` from `salary`.
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  salary_month,
  bonus
FROM salary;
sqlHints: All requested columns come from the `salary` table.
List the columns in the same order as the business request.

lesson.title: Return Unique Values
lesson.body:
HR teams often want a list of unique categories, such as departments, review outcomes, or promotion readiness values. Use DISTINCT when repeated values should appear once.

SELECT DISTINCT promotion_ready
FROM performance;

questions.question: You are supporting the talent manager, who wants to see the distinct promotion readiness categories used in the review table. Return unique `promotion_ready` values from `performance`.
questions.sqlStarterCode: -- Return each promotion_ready value only once
sqlHints: Use `DISTINCT` so duplicate values are removed.

questions.sectionDescription: Learn one filtering pattern at a time before combining anything more advanced.

lesson.title: Filter With One Condition
lesson.body:
Start filtering with one clear rule in the WHERE clause.

SELECT staff_id, performance_score
FROM performance
WHERE promotion_ready = 'Yes';

questions.question: You are an HR analyst preparing a shortlist for promotion review. Return `staff_id`, `performance_score`, and `promotion_ready` from `performance` for employees where `promotion_ready` is `Yes`.
questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  performance_score,
  promotion_ready
FROM performance
WHERE promotion_ready = '_ _ _';
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  performance_score,
  promotion_ready
FROM performance
WHERE promotion_ready = 'Yes';

lesson.title: Filter With AND
lesson.body:
Use AND when both conditions must be true.

SELECT staff_id, base_salary
FROM salary
WHERE base_salary >= 4000
  AND bonus >= 300;

questions.question: You are helping the payroll officer identify stronger earners for a bonus review. Return `staff_id`, `base_salary`, and `bonus` from `salary` where `base_salary` is at least `4000` and `bonus` is at least `300`.
questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  base_salary,
  bonus
FROM salary
WHERE base_salary >= 4000
  AND bonus >= _ _ _;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  base_salary,
  bonus
FROM salary
WHERE base_salary >= 4000
  AND bonus >= 300;
sqlHints: Both conditions must be true, so use `AND`.

lesson.title: Filter a Range With BETWEEN
lesson.body:
Use BETWEEN when a value needs to fall inside a range.

SELECT staff_id, performance_score
FROM performance
WHERE performance_score BETWEEN 4.0 AND 5.0;

questions.question: You are supporting the performance manager, who wants employees with strong review scores for calibration. Return `staff_id`, `performance_score`, and `training_hours` from `performance` where `performance_score` is between `4.0` and `5.0`.
questions.sqlStarterCode: -- Filter the performance table to the requested score range
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  performance_score,
  training_hours
FROM performance
WHERE performance_score BETWEEN 4.0 AND 5.0;

questions.sectionDescription: Only after retrieval and filtering do we introduce ordering and row limits.

lesson.title: Sort by One Column
lesson.body:
Use ORDER BY to control the sequence of the rows returned.

SELECT staff_id, base_salary
FROM salary
ORDER BY base_salary DESC;

questions.question: You are helping the compensation analyst rank current salaries from highest to lowest. Return `staff_id` and `base_salary` from `salary`, ordered by `base_salary` descending.
questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  base_salary
FROM salary
ORDER BY base_salary _ _ _;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  base_salary
FROM salary
ORDER BY base_salary DESC;

lesson.title: Sort by Multiple Columns
lesson.body:
When two rows may tie on one metric, add another sort rule after a comma.

SELECT staff_id, performance_score, absence_days
FROM performance
ORDER BY performance_score DESC, absence_days ASC;

questions.question: You are supporting the performance manager, who wants the review file sorted so the strongest scores appear first and, when scores are close, lower absence days appear first. Return `staff_id`, `performance_score`, and `absence_days` from `performance`, ordered by `performance_score` descending and `absence_days` ascending.
questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  performance_score,
  absence_days
FROM performance
ORDER BY performance_score DESC, absence_days ASC;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  performance_score,
  absence_days
FROM performance
ORDER BY performance_score DESC, absence_days ASC;
sqlHints: The first sort key is `performance_score DESC`.
The second sort key is `absence_days ASC`.

lesson.title: Limit the Number of Rows
lesson.body:
Use LIMIT when the business only wants the top few rows after sorting.

SELECT staff_id, base_salary
FROM salary
ORDER BY base_salary DESC
LIMIT 3;

questions.question: You are helping the HR director prepare a quick briefing on the top earners. Return `staff_id` and `base_salary` for the top 3 salaries in `salary`, ordered from highest to lowest base salary.
questions.sqlStarterCode: -- Return the three highest base salaries
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  base_salary
FROM salary
ORDER BY base_salary DESC
LIMIT 3;
sqlHints: The required number of rows is 3.

questions.sectionDescription: Turn raw HR values into payroll metrics and business-friendly labels.

lesson.title: Perform Arithmetic Operations
lesson.body:
SQL can calculate new HR metrics directly in the result.

SELECT staff_id, base_salary + bonus AS total_pay
FROM salary;

questions.question: You are supporting the payroll officer who wants a total monthly pay figure for each employee. Return `staff_id`, `base_salary`, `bonus`, and a calculated `total_pay` from `salary`.
questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  base_salary,
  bonus,
  base_salary + _ _ _ AS total_pay
FROM salary;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  base_salary,
  bonus,
  base_salary + bonus AS total_pay
FROM salary;

lesson.title: Apply String Functions
lesson.body:
You can format text in SQL so HR reports are easier to read.

SELECT
  first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name,
  UPPER(department) AS department_upper
FROM staff;

questions.question: You are helping the HR business partner prepare a cleaner employee directory. Return `staff_id`, a `full_name` column built from `first_name` and `last_name`, and `department_upper` using uppercase department names from `staff`.
questions.sqlStarterCode: -- Build a full name and uppercase department field
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name,
  UPPER(department) AS department_upper
FROM staff;
sqlHints: Use `||` to join the names with a space.

lesson.title: Apply Conditional Logic
lesson.body:
Use CASE when you need to convert a numeric HR metric into a simple business label.

SELECT
  staff_id,
  CASE
    WHEN performance_score >= 4.5 THEN 'Outstanding'
    ELSE 'Developing'
  END AS review_band
FROM performance;

questions.question: You are supporting the performance manager, who wants each review score grouped into a simple label. Return `staff_id`, `performance_score`, and `review_band` from `performance`. Use `Outstanding` for scores at least `4.5`, `Strong` for scores at least `4.0`, and `Developing` for all other scores.
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  staff_id,
  performance_score,
  CASE
    WHEN performance_score >= 4.5 THEN 'Outstanding'
    WHEN performance_score >= 4.0 THEN 'Strong'
    ELSE 'Developing'
  END AS review_band
FROM performance;

questions.sectionDescription: Summarize salary and performance data once the learner is ready for grouped analysis.

lesson.title: Calculate Column Summaries
lesson.body:
Aggregate functions summarize a whole column into one answer.

SELECT
  AVG(base_salary) AS avg_base_salary,
  MAX(base_salary) AS max_base_salary,
  MIN(base_salary) AS min_base_salary
FROM salary;

questions.question: You are helping the finance lead review the overall payroll picture. Return `avg_base_salary`, `max_base_salary`, and `min_base_salary` from `salary`. Round the average to 2 decimal places.
questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  ROUND(AVG(base_salary), 2) AS avg_base_salary,
  MAX(base_salary) AS max_base_salary,
  MIN(base_salary) AS min_base_salary
FROM salary;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  ROUND(AVG(base_salary), 2) AS avg_base_salary,
  MAX(base_salary) AS max_base_salary,
  MIN(base_salary) AS min_base_salary
FROM salary;
sqlHints: Round the average to 2 decimal places.

lesson.title: Group Data by Category
lesson.body:
Use GROUP BY when you need one summary per category, such as department.

SELECT st.department, ROUND(AVG(p.performance_score), 2)
FROM staff st
JOIN performance p ON st.staff_id = p.staff_id
GROUP BY st.department;

questions.question: You are supporting the HR manager, who wants average performance by department. Return `department` and `avg_performance_score`, rounded to 2 decimal places, by joining `staff` and `performance` and grouping by department.
questions.sqlStarterCode: -- Join staff to performance and calculate one average per department
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  st.department,
  ROUND(AVG(p.performance_score), 2) AS avg_performance_score
FROM staff st
JOIN performance p ON st.staff_id = p.staff_id
GROUP BY st.department;

lesson.title: Filter Aggregated Results With HAVING
lesson.body:
After you group data, use HAVING to filter the grouped results.

SELECT st.department, AVG(p.performance_score)
FROM staff st
JOIN performance p ON st.staff_id = p.staff_id
GROUP BY st.department
HAVING AVG(p.performance_score) >= 4.0;

questions.question: Which clause should you use to keep only grouped results where an aggregate like `AVG(performance_score)` meets a threshold?
questions.explanation: `HAVING` filters results after groups and aggregate values have already been calculated.
questions.hint: This clause is usually written after `GROUP BY`.

questions.question: You are preparing a summary for HR leadership and need only departments with an average performance score of at least `4.0`. Return `department` and `avg_performance_score`, rounded to 2 decimal places, from joined `staff` and `performance` data.
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  st.department,
  ROUND(AVG(p.performance_score), 2) AS avg_performance_score
FROM staff st
JOIN performance p ON st.staff_id = p.staff_id
GROUP BY st.department
HAVING AVG(p.performance_score) >= 4.0;
sqlHints: Build the grouped result first with `GROUP BY`.
